revision.text:
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]] A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[Japan]]ese round [[bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.
== History == The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[Han dynasty]], [[Three Kingdoms]], and [[Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[Lelang Commandery]] and [[Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}
== Variations == Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: *{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} *{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} *{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.
==See also== *[[Chinese magic mirror]] *[[TLV mirror]] *[[Yata no Kagami]]
==References== {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == {{refbegin}} * {{cite journal |last=Edwards |first=Walter |year=1998 |url=http://www.archaeology.org/9805/newsbriefs/japan.html |title= Mirrors to Japanese History |journal=Archaeology |volume=51 |issue=3}} * {{cite journal |last=Edwards |first=Walter |year=1999 |title=Mirrors on Ancient Yamato: The Kurozuka Kofun Discovery and the Question of Yamatai |journal=Monumenta Nipponica |volume=54 |issue=1 |pages=75–110|doi=10.2307/2668274 |jstor=2668274 }} * {{cite journal |last=Nishikawa |first=Toshikatsu |year=1999 |url=http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |title=Sankakubuchi shinjukyo (Triangular-rimmed mirrors) and the Mirrors Presented to Himiko |journal=Nihon Kōgaku |volume=8 |pages=87–99|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110131181906/http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |archive-date=2011-01-31 }} * {{cite book |editor1-last=Goodrich |editor1-first=Carrington C. |translator-last=Tsunoda |translator-first=Ryūsaku |translator-link=Ryūsaku Tsunoda |year=1951 |title=Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties |location=South Pasadena, California |publisher=P. D. and Ione Perkins}} {{refend}}
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.city.kobe.jp/cityoffice/57/maizoubunkazaic/relics/art/details/55.html 7号鏡 神人龍虎画像鏡] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071112221802/http://www.city.kobe.jp/cityoffice/57/maizoubunkazaic/relics/art/details/55.html |date=2007-11-12 }}, ''Shinjūkyō'' picturing a transcendent, dragon, and tiger, Kobe Archeology Center (click image for navigation) *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20020318155013/http://www.museum.kyoto-u.ac.jp/museumF/news/past/no5/kodai.html 三角縁神獣鏡], ''Sankakuen- shinjūkyō'' from the Yoshinogari site, Kyoto University Museum

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

==References==
{{reflist|20em}}

== Bibliography ==
{{refbegin}}
* {{cite journal |last=Edwards |first=Walter |year=1998 |url=http://www.archaeology.org/9805/newsbriefs/japan.html |title= Mirrors to Japanese History |journal=Archaeology |volume=51 |issue=3}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[d:" to "[[da:d:"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]

[[es:Shinju-kyo]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

revision.comment: Consolidate multiple wikidata link templates into single one at end
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

==References==
{{reflist|20em}}

== Bibliography ==
{{refbegin}}
* {{cite journal |last=Edwards |first=Walter |year=1998 |url=http://www.archaeology.org/9805/newsbriefs/japan.html |title= Mirrors to Japanese History |journal=Archaeology |volume=51 |issue=3}}
* {{cite journal |last=Edwards |first=Walter |year=1999 |title=Mirrors on Ancient Yamato: The Kurozuka Kofun Discovery and the Question of Yamatai |journal=Monumenta Nipponica |volume=54 |issue=1 |pages=75–110|doi=10.2307/2668274 |jstor=2668274 }}
* {{cite journal |last=Nishikawa |first=Toshikatsu |year=1999 |url=http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |title=Sankakubuchi shinjukyo (Triangular-rimmed mirrors) and the Mirrors Presented to Himiko |journal=Nihon Kōgaku |volume=8 |pages=87–99|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110131181906/http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |archive-date=2011-01-31 }}
* {{cite book |editor1-last=Goodrich |editor1-first=Carrington C. |translator-last=Tsunoda |translator-first=Ryūsaku |translator-link=Ryūsaku Tsunoda |year=1951 |title=Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties |location=South Pasadena, California |publisher=P. D. and Ione Perkins}}
{{refend}}

==External links==
*{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.city.kobe.jp/cityoffice/57/maizoubunkazaic/relics/art/details/55.html 7号鏡 神人龍虎画像鏡] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071112221802/http://www.city.kobe.jp/cityoffice/57/maizoubunkazaic/relics/art/details/55.html |date=2007-11-12 }}, ''Shinjūkyō'' picturing a transcendent, dragon, and tiger, Kobe Archeology Center (click image for navigation)
*{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20020318155013/http://www.museum.kyoto-u.ac.jp/museumF/news/past/no5/kodai.html 三角縁神獣鏡], ''Sankakuen- shinjūkyō'' from the Yoshinogari site, Kyoto University Museum

{{refend}}

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

D. and Ione Perkins}}
{{refend}}

The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7497614]]
Wikidata name: shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in [[:en:Yamashiro, Kyoto|Yamashiro, Kyoto]]]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese round [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] decorated with images of gods and animals from [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese mythology]]. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xiān]]'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the [[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirrors]] and was frequently produced during the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]], [[:en:Three Kingdoms|Three Kingdoms]], and [[:en:Six Dynasties|Six Dynasties]] (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daifang Commandery]] in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''[[:en:Records of the Three Kingdoms|Records of the Three Kingdoms]]'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor [[:en:Cao Rui|Cao Rui]] sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*[[:en:Chinese magic mirror|Chinese magic mirror]]
*[[:en:TLV mirror|TLV mirror]]
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

* {{cite journal |last=Nishikawa |first=Toshikatsu |year=1999 |url=http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |title=Sankakubuchi shinjukyo (Triangular-rimmed mirrors) and the Mirrors Presented to Himiko |journal=Nihon Kōgaku |volume=8 |pages=87–99|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110131181906/http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |archive-date=2011-01-31 }}

D. and Ione Perkins}}
{{refend}}

The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xiān}}'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the {{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirrors}} and was frequently produced during the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty}}, {{ill|Three Kingdoms|en|Three Kingdoms|WD=Q185043|lt=Three Kingdoms}}, and {{ill|Six Dynasties|en|Six Dynasties|WD=Q1197281|lt=Six Dynasties}} (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the {{ill|Lelang Commandery|en|Lelang Commandery|WD=Q701376|lt=Lelang Commandery}} and {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|WD=Q704994|lt=Daifang Commandery}} in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''{{ill|Records of the Three Kingdoms|en|Records of the Three Kingdoms|WD=Q247318|lt=Records of the Three Kingdoms}}'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui}} sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}} contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*{{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirror}}
*{{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=TLV mirror}}
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}} contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*{{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirror}}
*{{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=TLV mirror}}
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

{{reflist|20em}}

D. and Ione Perkins}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]

{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in {{ill|Yamashiro, Kyoto|en|Yamashiro, Kyoto|WD=Q8047935|lt=Yamashiro, Kyoto}}]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese round {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} decorated with images of gods and animals from {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|WD=Q210677|lt=Chinese mythology}}. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xiān}}'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the {{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirrors}} and was frequently produced during the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty}}, {{ill|Three Kingdoms|en|Three Kingdoms|WD=Q185043|lt=Three Kingdoms}}, and {{ill|Six Dynasties|en|Six Dynasties|WD=Q1197281|lt=Six Dynasties}} (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the {{ill|Lelang Commandery|en|Lelang Commandery|WD=Q701376|lt=Lelang Commandery}} and {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|WD=Q704994|lt=Daifang Commandery}} in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''{{ill|Records of the Three Kingdoms|en|Records of the Three Kingdoms|WD=Q247318|lt=Records of the Three Kingdoms}}'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui}} sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}} contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*{{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirror}}
*{{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=TLV mirror}}
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

==References==
{{reflist|20em}}

== Bibliography ==
{{refbegin}}

* {{cite journal |last=Edwards |first=Walter |year=1999 |title=Mirrors on Ancient Yamato: The Kurozuka Kofun Discovery and the Question of Yamatai |journal=Monumenta Nipponica |volume=54 |issue=1 |pages=75–110|doi=10.2307/2668274 |jstor=2668274 }}
* {{cite journal |last=Nishikawa |first=Toshikatsu |year=1999 |url=http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |title=Sankakubuchi shinjukyo (Triangular-rimmed mirrors) and the Mirrors Presented to Himiko |journal=Nihon Kōgaku |volume=8 |pages=87–99|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110131181906/http://wwwsoc.nii.ac.jp/jaa2/journal/con8abs.html |archive-date=2011-01-31 }}
* {{cite book |editor1-last=Goodrich |editor1-first=Carrington C. |translator-last=Tsunoda |translator-first=Ryūsaku |translator-link=Ryūsaku Tsunoda |year=1951 |title=Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties |location=South Pasadena, California |publisher=P. D. and Ione Perkins}}
{{refend}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinju-kyo]][[es:Shinju-kyo]][[fr:Shinjū-kyō]][[ja:神獣鏡]][[uk:Сіндзю-кьо]][[zh:神獸鏡]]

{{Short description|Ancient type of Japanese bronze mirror}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
[[File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|thumb|''Sankakuen-shinjūkyō'' from the Tsubai Ōtsukayama kofun in {{ill|Yamashiro, Kyoto|en|Yamashiro, Kyoto|WD=Q8047935|lt=Yamashiro, Kyoto|1=Yamashiro, Kyoto}}]]
A {{nihongo|'''''Shinjū-kyō'''''|神獣鏡||"deity and beast mirror"}} is an ancient type of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese round {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} decorated with images of gods and animals from {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|WD=Q210677|lt=Chinese mythology|1=Chinese mythology}}. The obverse side has a polished mirror and the reverse has relief representations of legendary Chinese ''[[:en:Shen (Chinese religion)|shén]]'' ({{lang|zh|神}} "spirit; god"), ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xiān|1=Xian (Taoism)}}'' ({{lang|zh|仙}} "transcendent; immortal"), and legendary creatures.

== History ==
The {{transl|ja|shinjū-kyō}} style of bronze mirror originated from the {{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirrors|1=Chinese magic mirror}} and was frequently produced during the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}, {{ill|Three Kingdoms|en|Three Kingdoms|WD=Q185043|lt=Three Kingdoms|1=Three Kingdoms}}, and {{ill|Six Dynasties|en|Six Dynasties|WD=Q1197281|lt=Six Dynasties|1=Six Dynasties}} (1st–6th centuries CE). With the spread of Chinese bronze casting technology, {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}} were also produced in Japan and the {{ill|Lelang Commandery|en|Lelang Commandery|WD=Q701376|lt=Lelang Commandery|1=Lelang Commandery}} and {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|WD=Q704994|lt=Daifang Commandery|1=Daifang Commandery}} in the Korean peninsula. The {{circa|297}} {{transl|zh|Wei zhi}} ({{lang|zh|魏志}} "Records of Wei"), which is part of the ''{{ill|Records of the Three Kingdoms|en|Records of the Three Kingdoms|WD=Q247318|lt=Records of the Three Kingdoms|1=Records of the Three Kingdoms}}'' ({{lang|zh|三國志}}), has the first historical reference to bronze mirrors in Japan. It chronicles tributary relations between Queen [[Himiko]] of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] and the Wei court, and records that in 239, Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui|1=Cao Rui}} sent presents to Himiko, including "one hundred bronze mirrors".{{sfnp|Goodrich|1951|p=15}}

== Variations ==
Archeological excavations of Japanese tombs from the [[Kofun period]] (3rd–7th centuries) have revealed numerous {{transl|ja|shinjūkyō}}, and Japanese archeologists divide them into subtypes including: 
*{{nihongo|''sankakuen-shinjūkyō''|三角縁神獣鏡||"triangular-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}}
*{{nihongo|''gamontai-shinjūkyō''|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡||"wide image-band deity and beast mirror"}} 
*{{nihongo|''hirabuchi-shinjūkyō''|平縁神獣鏡||"flat-rimmed deity and beast mirror"}} 
Kurotsuka ''[[kofun]]'' tomb excavated in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}} contained 33 {{transl|ja|sankakuen-shinjūkyō}} bronze mirrors. Some scholars{{sfnp|Edwards|1998}}{{sfnp|Edwards|1999}}{{sfnp|Nishikawa|1999}} believe they are the original mirrors that Emperor Cao Rui presented to Queen Himiko, but others disagree.

==See also==
*{{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirror|1=Chinese magic mirror}}
*{{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=TLV mirror|1=TLV mirror}}
*[[Yata no Kagami]]

D. and Ione Perkins}}
{{refend}}
